section.title: Foundations
section.intro: The essays and books that shaped the AI conversation. Widely cited, frequently disagreeing with each other.

resources.title: ChatGPT Is a Blurry JPEG of the Web
resources.author: Ted Chiang
resources.description: Language models as lossy compression of the internet. Whether or not the analogy holds technically, it gave non-specialists a way to think about what these systems are doing.

resources.title: Will A.I. Become the New McKinsey?
resources.author: Ted Chiang
resources.description: AI as management consulting: a tool that lets organizations do things they already wanted to do while diffusing accountability.

resources.title: Why A.I. Isn't Going to Make Art
resources.author: Ted Chiang
resources.description: Art requires the choices a human makes. Contested, but it names the unease people feel watching AI approximate creative work.

resources.title: Machines of Loving Grace
resources.author: Dario Amodei
resources.description: Anthropic's CEO on what AI could do for biology, health, economic development, and governance if development goes well. The optimistic case from someone building the systems.

resources.title: The Adolescence of Technology
resources.author: Dario Amodei
resources.description: Amodei on the governance challenge of systems that are capable enough to be dangerous but not yet reliable enough to trust. 20,000 words.

resources.title: Situational Awareness: The Decade Ahead
resources.author: Leopold Aschenbrenner
resources.description: 165 pages making the accelerationist case: AGI by 2027 is plausible, superintelligence follows shortly. Aschenbrenner turned this essay into a $1.5 billion hedge fund. Represents the worldview driving tens of billions in AI investment.

resources.title: AI Snake Oil
resources.author: Arvind Narayanan & Sayash Kapoor
resources.description: Distinguishes what AI can do from what it's marketed as doing, built from research on prediction limits and the reproducibility crisis in ML-based science. The companion blog (renamed AI as Normal Technology) frames AI as useful, flawed, and needing regulation.

resources.title: The Shape of AI: Jaggedness, Bottlenecks and Salients
resources.author: Ethan Mollick
resources.description: Where AI capability is advancing (salients), where it's stuck (bottlenecks), and why the frontier stays uneven (jaggedness).

resources.title: Mass Intelligence
resources.author: Ethan Mollick
resources.description: Over 1 billion people now use AI tools. Asks what happens to institutions built around the premise that expertise is rare when generating competent text and analysis is effectively free.

resources.title: Re-sourcing the Mind
resources.author: L.M. Sacasas
resources.description: When AI drafts the email, writes the memo, and summarizes the meeting, the labor of putting thought into words – often the process that IS the thinking – is lost.

resources.title: Reverse Centaurs and Enshittification
resources.author: Cory Doctorow
resources.description: The "reverse centaur" inverts the human-AI partnership: instead of AI augmenting human judgment, humans service AI systems – monitoring, correcting, providing judgment while being paid as if the machine did the work.

resources.title: Home-Cooked Software and Barefoot Developers
resources.author: Maggie Appleton
resources.description: AI as a tool for enabling anyone to build small, personal, useful software. Counterpoint to the deskilling narrative.

resources.title: Blood in the Machine
resources.author: Brian Merchant
resources.description: History of the Luddites – skilled workers fighting the use of machinery to drive down wages, not technophobes. The ongoing newsletter includes an "AI Killed My Job" series.

resources.title: Empire of AI
resources.author: Karen Hao
resources.description: Investigative journalism tracing AI's supply chain from rare earth mines to training data labelers to deployment decisions.
